reviews.review_text:
Total War: Attila is probably the grimmest, most oppressive, and weirdly beautiful strategy game Creative Assembly ever made.

Most strategy games make expansion feel inevitable. Attila makes collapse feel inevitable, and that completely changes the psychology of how you play. You are not building a shining empire at the height of civilization. You are trying to survive the slow-motion end of the world while everything catches fire around you.

And honestly? That atmosphere is incredible.

The Western Roman Empire campaign alone is one of the greatest strategy experiences ever created if you’re the kind of person who enjoys stress as a recreational activity. Half your provinces are starving, your armies are overstretched, public order is collapsing, sanitation is apparently a forgotten science, and every neighboring tribe smells weakness. The game constantly forces hard tradeoffs because you simply cannot save everything.

What makes Attila special though is how well the mechanics reinforce the theme. Climate deterioration, migration, food shortages, religious instability, razed settlements, disease, all of it contributes to this relentless sense that civilization is fraying at the edges. You don’t just read about decline in flavor text. You feel it mechanically every turn.

The battles are also some of the best in the Total War series because they feel brutal and desperate. Cavalry charges hit hard, defensive battles feel meaningful, and fire genuinely changes engagements in terrifying ways. Watching a city burn while exhausted troops try to hold collapsing streets against waves of attackers still looks incredible years later.

And unlike some other Total War games, Attila benefits massively from restraint in tone. It doesn’t romanticize the era. It feels cold, exhausted, and unstable. There’s this constant undercurrent that the old world is dying and nobody really knows what replaces it.

It also might have the best campaign storytelling in the series despite barely “telling” a story conventionally. The narrative emerges naturally from survival. The abandoned frontier province you sacrificed to save Italy. The last veteran legion holding a river crossing. The desperate migration to more defensible territory. Few strategy games create stories that feel this historically grounded and personal at the same time.

Sure, the optimization can still occasionally behave like it’s personally offended by your CPU, and some systems are unapologetically punishing. But honestly, that friction almost fits the game. Attila is not interested in making you comfortable.

It’s a strategy game about endurance, decline, and surviving history when history has decided it no longer cares about your plans. Absolute masterpiece.
A great standalone game plus there are mods to change it to Lord of the Rings when you get tired of the main game.
This game is so fun! I've been playing as the Western Romans for so long. Its a fun challenge winning on legendary.
I tried booting up this game after quite some time again and all it did was stutter. Just goes to show how CA maintains their quality standards.

I cannot recommend this game. I played 1.5k hours in rome2, and this game has the same flaws as that one. I just cannot enjoy having ``abilities`` that gives stats. I prefer Medieval2 way. example you form a wedge formation for cavalry no stats change except your cavalry will move slower yet have a strong punch power. In Attila though -50 mass entity, lower speed, more charge damage. Stats based game, not common sense.

And a ton of other  small issues like gate opening in a siege like wtf? And you cannot ram it when it does. In med2 gate stays open unless you sally out. Performance also sucks big time in this game. For me it was a loss of 20 euros, and that s fine. Gonna give it a bad review. 

If you like this kind of games, try Medieval2, Shogun2, and Napoleon total war. Those are worth trying and be played. Not whatever this game is.

Honestly it is a lot of fun and it is quite difficult from the traditional experience of a total war game, fighting battles is interesting and can be quite different for different factions. The mod community is great
How is it possible that this game is so amazing yet pisses me off so much? makes me wanna throw my laptop out of a moving car.
great game play and mechanics, easy to play but hard enough to keep you interested

This game is only alive because of its mods
otherwise game has many issues and its not funny like Total war Three kingdoms
This game aged like milk, I got such a low performance and fps drops it's insane and I know there are fixes but I'm not doing CA's work, this game is pathetic at this point.
It is so slow on my laptop I can't play it.  I've gone through all the fixes I've found online and nothing works.  I really wanted to try the LoTR mod for it.

I have played total war since Rome one came out and this is by far my favorite of the series. I played vanilla for a handful of hours. The rest has been mods like 1212AD makes buying totally worth it.
If this game was historically accurate Attila the hun would have had flowing blonde hair and blue eyes the colour of the atlantic ocean.

Total War Attila me parece el mejor de todos los Total War. He jugado a muchos de ellos, desde el Napoleon hasta el Three Kingdoms y este los supera a todos. La época histórica, justo durante la caída del Imperio Romano de Occidente, el daño de las grandes migraciones en territorio romano, simplemente expectacular.

He jugado tanto con mods como en el juego base (vanilla), y de ambas formas me ha gustado mucho.

La ambientación y apartado visual sigue siendo impresionante actualmente y el mapa de campaña es, sencillamente, precioso. Los discursos de los oficiales antes de la batalla, seguidos por el grito de guerra al unísono de todo el ejército... es algo absolutamente épico que te pone los pelos de punta. La diversidad de facciones y unidades jugables le da una rejugabilidad infinita.

El tema del rendimiento:
Es verdad que cuando salió al mercado el juego sufría bastante de FPS, especialmente cuando se empezaban a acumular los cadáveres en el campo de batalla. Sin embargo, a día de hoy el hardware actual ha eliminado ese problema por completo. Ahora las batallas masivas se disfrutan y se ven espectaculares sin tirones.

Si consiguieron hacer esta obra de arte con la caída de Roma, tengo muchísimas ganas de ver qué son capaces de hacer cuando saquen el Medieval 3.
Playing as the Western Roman Empire is the ultimate "Tower Defense" Grand Strategy!
Just Bought it For 1212AD LOL But Anyway the Mod Alone Makes The Game Worth it.

Total War Three Kingdoms at least let me play for a bit until towards the end of the game before it crashes, but this trash doesn't even let me start.
